note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old male presents with a one-week history of burning urination without fever, flank pain, or any genital discharge. He denies systemic symptoms such as chills or malaise. Patient is not pregnant. There are no reported allergies.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings cannot be assessed. No urine dipstick or culture results are available at this time.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis)  
  - Differential: Urethritis (non-gonococcal or gonococcal), prostatitis, sexually transmitted infection  
  - Red flags considered: No fever or flank pain to suggest pyelonephritis; no discharge to suggest STI; no systemic symptoms indicating complicated infection

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage in urgent care or primary care setting with close follow-up if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy with nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days, assuming no contraindications; alternatively, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if local resistance patterns allow and no sulfa allergy. Encourage increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief with NSAIDs or acetaminophen. Advise urine culture if symptoms persist beyond treatment or worsen.  
  - Safety net: Return promptly for evaluation if fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, hematuria, or inability to urinate develops, or if symptoms do not improve within 48–72 hours of antibiotic initiation.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration: 1 week  
- Sex at birth: male  
- Fever: no  
- Flank pain: no  
- Discharge: no  
- Pregnancy: no (Patient is not pregnant)
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
